stem: How many distinct Hamiltonian cycles does the complete graph \( K_{[[n]]} \) have? (Two cycles are considered the same if they consist of the same set of edges, so rotations and reflections are not counted separately.)
solution: Fix a starting vertex; the remaining \( n-1 \) vertices can be ordered in \( (n-1)! \) ways, but each cycle is counted twice (once in each direction), giving \( \frac{(n-1)!}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets that each cycle is traversed in two directions; divide by 2.
This fixes no starting vertex, overcounting by a factor of \( n \) before halving.
This counts all \( n! \) orderings of the vertices as distinct.
This uses \( (n-2)! \), dropping a factor from the arrangement count.
This counts the edges of \( K_n \), not its Hamiltonian cycles.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: How many distinct Hamiltonian PATHS (paths visiting every vertex exactly once, with two endpoints rather than a closed loop) does the complete graph \( K_{[[n]]} \) have? (A path and its reverse traversal are considered the same path.)
solution: A Hamiltonian path is an ordering of all \( n \) vertices, and there are \( n! \) such orderings; but each path is counted twice, once for each direction of traversal, giving \( \frac{n!}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets that traversing a path forward and backward gives the same path; divide by 2.
This is the Hamiltonian CYCLE count formula, which fixes a vertex since a cycle has no endpoints; a path does not work this way.
This fixes a starting vertex as if for a cycle, but a path has two distinguishable endpoints, not a fixed rotation point.
This drops an extra factor from the arrangement count.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: How many distinct DIRECTED Hamiltonian cycles does the complete graph \( K_{[[n]]} \) have, if a cycle traversed clockwise is considered DIFFERENT from the same cycle traversed counterclockwise (but rotations to a different starting vertex along the same direction are considered the same cycle)?
solution: Fix a starting vertex; the remaining \( n-1 \) vertices can be ordered in \( (n-1)! \) ways, and now each such ordering gives a DIFFERENT cycle since direction matters (no division by 2). So the count is \( (n-1)! = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by 2 for direction, but here clockwise and counterclockwise are explicitly counted as different.
This counts all \( n! \) orderings of the vertices without fixing a starting vertex to remove rotational duplicates.
This is the undirected-cycle count, dividing by 2 for direction, which does not apply here.
This uses \( (n-2)! \), dropping a factor from the arrangement count.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.
